summary: American modernist poet, best known for the poem "Poetry" which begins "I, too, dislike it" and contains the phrase "imaginary gardens with real toads in them." Also known for animal poems like "The Fish," "The Pangolin," and "An Octopus," and for mentoring Elizabeth Bishop. Editor of The Dial. Known for syllabic verse.

works.name: Poetry
works.description: Moore's most famous poem, frequently cited in quizbowl. Begins with the declaration "I, too, dislike it." Contains the famous phrase "imaginary gardens with real toads in them" and invokes "literalists of the imagination." Lists "the baseball fan" and "the statistician" among "things that are important beyond all this fiddle." Refuses to discriminate against "business documents and school-books." Notoriously cut from 29 lines to just 3 lines in her 1967 Complete Poems, prefaced by the epigraph "Omissions are not accidents." Also references "hands that can grasp, eyes that can dilate."

works.name: The Fish
works.description: A poem describing a cliffside as a "defiant edifice" with "all the physical features of ac-cident" where creatures "wade through black jade." Shares its title with a well-known Elizabeth Bishop poem. Moore uses enjambed syllabic verse and apocopated rhyme (e.g., rhyming "lack" with the "ac-" of "accident"). Notes that "the sea grows old in it."

works.name: The Steeple-Jack
works.description: A poem set in a peaceful seaside town with "eight stranded whales to look at" where the title figure C. J. Poole puts up a red-and-white "Danger" sign. Includes the college student Ambrose reading "not-native books." Moore remarks that "Durer would have seen a reason for living" in this town. Opens her 1967 Complete Poems.

works.name: An Octopus
works.description: A poem inspired by Moore's trip to Mount Rainier in Washington state. Begins "of ice. Deceptively reserved and flat" and uses the title creature (an octopus of ice, i.e., a glacier) to describe the peak. References "the white volcano with no weather side; lightning flashing at its base." Includes Greeks who "resolve with benevolent conclusiveness 'complexities which will still be complexities as long as the world lasts.'"

works.name: Marriage
works.description: A long poem collected in Observations. Describes marriage as "an institution... requiring public promises of one's intention." Includes a conversation where a woman says "I should like to be alone" and a man replies "why not be alone together?" Criticizes men as "monopolists of stars, garters, buttons, and other shining baubles / unfit to be the guardians of another person's happiness." Ends with "the book on the writing table / the hand in the breast-pocket" and quotes Daniel Webster about "enterprise."

works.name: The Pangolin
works.description: A poem praising the title "armored animal," described as "a near artichoke with head and legs and grit-equipped gizzard." Compares the pangolin to "Leonardo da Vinci's replica" and a "night miniature artist engineer." Ends with "Again the sun! anew each day."

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Moore was a major American modernist poet associated with Imagism and syllabic verse. She mentored Elizabeth Bishop and edited The Dial magazine in the 1920s. Known as a baseball fan (Brooklyn Dodgers) who compared baseball to writing. Collaborated with Muhammad Ali on a poem about Ernie Terrell. Proposed car names like "Mongoose Civique" and "Utopian Turtletop" for Ford. Translated La Fontaine's Fables. Used unaccented syllabic verse. Also wrote "To a Snail," "To a Steam Roller," "To a Chameleon," "The Mind Is an Enchanting Thing," "Nevertheless," "A Grave," "England," "O to Be a Dragon," and "Baseball and Writing."

comprehensive_summary:
Marianne Moore is one of the most frequently clued American modernist poets in quizbowl. Her signature poem is "Poetry," which begins with the iconic declaration "I, too, dislike it" and contains the famous phrase "imaginary gardens with real toads in them." The poem invokes "literalists of the imagination" and lists "the baseball fan," "the immovable critic," and "the statistician" among phenomena we do not admire because we do not understand them. Moore controversially cut the poem from 29 lines to just 3 in her 1967 Complete Poems, which opens with the epigraph "Omissions are not accidents."

Moore's animal poems are another major focus of clues. "The Fish" describes creatures that "wade through black jade" at a cliffside described as a "defiant edifice," and shares its title with a famous Elizabeth Bishop poem. "The Pangolin" praises the title "armored animal" as "a near artichoke with head and legs" and compares it to "Leonardo da Vinci's replica." "An Octopus" was inspired by a trip to Mount Rainier and begins "of ice. Deceptively reserved and flat," using the glacier as its central metaphor.

Moore's "The Steeple-Jack" is set in a coastal town with "eight stranded whales" where C. J. Poole puts up a "Danger" sign and gilds a "solid-pointed star, which stands for hope." The poem notes that "Durer would have seen a reason for living" there. Her long poem "Marriage" describes the institution as requiring "public promises of one's intention" and criticizes men as "monopolists of stars, garters, buttons."

Biographically, Moore mentored Elizabeth Bishop and edited The Dial magazine in the 1920s, where she rejected Hart Crane's work. She was a devoted baseball fan (Brooklyn Dodgers) and wrote "Baseball and Writing." She collaborated with Muhammad Ali on a poem about Ernie Terrell and proposed unusual car names for Ford. She was a master of unaccented syllabic verse and translated La Fontaine's Fables.

Poem: inspired by Mount Rainier; begins "of ice. Deceptively reserved and flat" and uses a glacier as a metaphor for the title creature
Poem: describes the title creature as "a near artichoke with head and legs" and a "night miniature artist engineer" compared to "Leonardo da Vinci's replica"
Poem: a woman says "I should like to be alone" and a man replies "why not be alone together?" — ends with "the book on the writing table; the hand in the breast-pocket"

Author: collaborated with Muhammad Ali on "Poem on the Annihilation of Ernie Terrell" and praised him as the "smiling pugilist"
